taskDescription: Click on the key anatomical terms related to the arm and cubital fossa in the text below.
textField:
The arm is divided into anterior and posterior compartments by the medial and lateral *intermuscular* *septa*. The anterior compartment contains three muscles: *biceps* *brachii*, *brachialis*, and *coracobrachialis*. These are all supplied by the *musculocutaneous* nerve (C5, C6, C7). The posterior compartment contains only the *triceps* *brachii*, supplied by the *radial* nerve.

The *radial* nerve runs in the *spiral* *groove* of the humerus along with the profunda brachii artery. Compression here causes *Saturday* *night* *paralysis*, resulting in *wrist* *drop* due to loss of wrist and finger extension.

The *cubital* *fossa* is a triangular depression anterior to the elbow. Its lateral boundary is *brachioradialis*, its medial boundary is *pronator* *teres*, and the floor is formed by *brachialis* and *supinator*. The *brachial* artery lies in the fossa and bifurcates into the *radial* and *ulnar* arteries. The *median* *cubital* vein crosses superficially and is the preferred site for *venipuncture*.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the arm and cubital fossa anatomy and try again.
